title: Replace ticket-derived tx_id generation with issued transaction IDs
summary: Implement the 0.6.0 runtime identity simplification that replaces ticket-derived transaction identifiers with canonical issued transaction IDs. This ticket updates the transaction-begin issuance path so new runtime work always receives a monotonic server-managed `tx_id`, while resume, rebuild, verify, commit, and repair continuation paths always reuse the existing active transaction identity. The goal is to make transaction identity exact, boring, and interruption-safe so the work loop can continue the same durable execution attempt without heuristic replacement.
outputs: Runtime code that issues new monotonic `tx_id` values only during canonical transaction begin
Runtime code that stops deriving `tx_id` from `ticket_id` in normal new-write behavior
Runtime behavior that preserves exact active transaction identity across resume, verify, commit, and repair continuation
Updated validation paths that distinguish transaction identity from planning identity
Updated compatibility handling for historical legacy transaction IDs during replay and rebuild
Regression coverage for issued transaction identity behavior in runtime code
acceptance_criteria: New transaction creation no longer derives `tx_id` from `ticket_id` in normal runtime behavior.
Canonical transaction begin allocates a new monotonic issued `tx_id` using the documented issuance path.
Resume paths reuse the existing active `tx_id` and do not mint a replacement transaction identity.
Rebuild paths preserve historical transaction identity and do not synthesize new transaction IDs for existing history.
Verify, commit, and repair continuation flows do not mint new `tx_id` values for the active transaction.
The implementation does not start a new transaction while a non-terminal active transaction exists.
Runtime comparison logic no longer relies on ticket-name-derived transaction identity in normal flow.
Historical legacy transaction IDs remain replayable as opaque historical identifiers.
Newly issued transaction IDs use the documented JSON-facing representation consistently.
Regression tests cover at least: new transaction issuance, resume reuse, no replacement IDs during continuation, and compatibility with legacy historical IDs.
The implementation preserves canonical lifecycle ordering, resumability, replay integrity, and explicit terminal-state handling.
tasks: Identify all runtime code paths that currently derive, infer, or normalize transaction identity from `ticket_id` during new transaction creation.
Update canonical transaction-begin logic so it allocates a new monotonic issued `tx_id` through the documented issuance path.
Ensure canonical transaction begin is the only normal new-write path that can allocate a fresh `tx_id`.
Ensure resume logic always reuses the exact active `tx_id` rather than minting or inferring a replacement identifier.
Ensure verify, commit, and repair continuation logic preserve the existing active transaction identity.
Ensure rebuild logic treats historical transaction IDs as opaque historical data rather than forcing them into the new issuance format.
Update runtime validation and comparison logic so `ticket_id` and `tx_id` are handled as distinct identities with distinct roles.
Remove or refactor runtime assumptions that transaction identity should embed ticket meaning in normal flow.
Update any serialization or materialized-state logic needed so newly issued transaction identifiers are stored consistently in JSON-facing artifacts.
Add or update regression tests for new issued transaction creation, exact transaction reuse during resume, and compatibility with legacy historical transaction IDs.
Document any bounded compatibility exceptions required so old histories remain replayable without weakening the new-write policy.
notes: This ticket is runtime implementation work, not only contract-definition work.
The most important outcome is that transaction identity becomes exact and stable across interruption, not semantically meaningful by name shape.
This ticket should simplify runtime behavior by narrowing the only valid issuance implementation path for new transaction identity.
Historical replay compatibility matters, but it must not dilute the strictness of the new-write policy.
The implementation should favor explicit exact identity checks over heuristics, normalization tricks, or ticket-based inference.

implementation_scope.primary_focus: Runtime replacement of ticket-derived transaction identity with issued transaction IDs.
implementation_scope.non_goal: Rewriting all historical transaction identities into the new format.

identity_rules.ticket_id: Planning identity for the work item and not the runtime execution identifier.
identity_rules.tx_id: Exact runtime transaction identity for one durable execution attempt.
identity_rules.new_write_policy: All newly created transactions use issued monotonic `tx_id` values through the canonical transaction-begin issuance path.
identity_rules.historical_policy: Historical legacy transaction IDs remain readable as opaque historical identifiers.

issuance_rules: Only canonical transaction begin may mint a new `tx_id`.
A fresh `tx_id` must not be issued while a non-terminal active transaction already exists.
Resume must reuse the current active `tx_id`.
Rebuild must not mint replacement transaction IDs for existing history.
Verify, commit, and repair continuation must not mint new transaction IDs.

compatibility_rules.legacy_replay: Legacy transaction IDs remain replayable as historical state.
compatibility_rules.no_forced_rewrite: Forward migration does not require in-place rewrite of historical event logs.
compatibility_rules.new_runtime_behavior: Normal runtime behavior must stop deriving transaction identity from ticket identity.

test_focus: begin issues a new monotonic transaction ID
resume reuses the same active transaction ID
continuation steps do not mint replacement IDs

design_bias.preferred_anchor: Exact active transaction identity issued once through canonical begin and reused until terminal completion.
avoid: issuing a fresh transaction while non-terminal active work already exists
rewriting legacy historical identifiers during normal runtime flow

canonical_alignment.synchronization_intent: Use this ticket to make runtime transaction identity match the 0.6.0 transaction-centered resumable protocol by implementing the canonical issuance path and exact active-transaction reuse behavior.
canonical_alignment.current_canonical_constraint: Implementation must preserve the single-active-transaction model, must not issue a fresh transaction while non-terminal active work exists, and must preserve exact active transaction continuation defined by the earlier 0.6.0 contract tickets.
